headline: Super Micro Co-Founder Charged in China AI Tech Case
summary: US prosecutors charged Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw in a scheme to send high-performance computer servers assembled in the US with sophisticated AI capabilities to China in violation of US export controls.  Liaw and others at the company allegedly sold the AI tech through a Southeast Asia company knowing it would be sent on to China.

Liaw and others at the company allegedly sold the AI tech through a Southeast Asia company knowing it would be sent on to China.

headline: Super Micro Indictment Puts Focus On Compliance Risks And Valuation Gap
summary: US authorities have indicted a co founder of Super Micro Computer and several employees for alleged export control violations tied to AI capable servers shipped to China. The individuals are accused of sending advanced server systems to restricted Chinese customers in breach of US export laws. Super Micro Computer is not named as a defendant, and the company has placed the implicated employees on leave and ended work with a contractor while cooperating with investigators. For investors...

headline: Super Micro shares plunge as US charges co-founder, 2 more for smuggling AI chips to China
summary: Super Micro shares sank 24% on Friday after U.S. prosecutors charged three people linked with the company, including its co-founder, ‌with helping smuggle billions of dollars worth of AI technology to ‌China.  The drop in premarket trading would erase more than $4 billion from Super Micro's $18.49 billion market value ​if the losses hold.  U.S. prosecutors did not name Super Micro - a major AI server builder using Nvidia's chips - in the complaint.

The drop in premarket trading would erase more than $4 billion from Super Micro's $18.49 billion market value ​if the losses hold.  U.S. prosecutors did not name Super Micro - a major AI server builder using Nvidia's chips - in the complaint.

headline: Super Micro Shares Sink 24% After U.S. Charges Co-Founder Over Alleged Chip-Smuggling Scheme
summary: Shares of Super Micro Computer are heading for one of their biggest daily drops ever after U.S. prosecutors charged three people, including the company's co-founder, with smuggling machines with high-end Nvidia chips to China.  Shares were recently down 24% premarket, putting the server maker on track to shed about $4.5 billion from its market capitalization of $18.5 billion as of Thursday's close.  In an indictment unsealed Thursday, prosecutors accused the three people of helping smuggle servers into China “through a tangled web of lies, obfuscation, and concealment—all to drive sales and generate revenues in violation of U.S. law."

In an indictment unsealed Thursday, prosecutors accused the three people of helping smuggle servers into China “through a tangled web of lies, obfuscation, and concealment—all to drive sales and generate revenues in violation of U.S. law."

headline: Super Micro Scandal Is a Big Win for Dell Stock
summary: Dell Technologies  stock surged Friday on news that had nothing to do with Dell.  The PC and server company got a boost after the Justice Department charged three individuals associated with  Super Micro Computer  Dell’s arch-rival, for allegedly skirting export-control laws by diverting $2.5 billion worth of U.S.-assembled servers to China.  One of the defendants is Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Super Micro’s co-founder and senior vice president, as well as a board member.

content.title: Super Micro Scandal Is a Big Win for Dell Stock
content.summary: Dell Technologies  stock surged Friday on news that had nothing to do with Dell.  The PC and server company got a boost after the Justice Department charged three individuals associated with  Super Micro Computer  Dell’s arch-rival, for allegedly skirting export-control laws by diverting $2.5 billion worth of U.S.-assembled servers to China.  One of the defendants is Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Super Micro’s co-founder and senior vice president, as well as a board member.

headline: Super Micro Stock Sinks After U.S. Charges Co-Founder
summary: Shares of Super Micro Computer are heading for one of their biggest daily drops ever after U.S. prosecutors charged three people, including the company's co-founder, with smuggling machines with high-end Nvidia chips to China.  Shares were recently down 26%, putting the server maker on track to shed about $4.5 billion from its market capitalization of $18.5 billion as of Thursday's close.  In an indictment unsealed Thursday, prosecutors accused the three people of helping smuggle servers into China “through a tangled web of lies, obfuscation, and concealment—all to drive sales and generate revenues in violation of U.S. law."

In an indictment unsealed Thursday, prosecutors accused the three people of helping smuggle servers into China “through a tangled web of lies, obfuscation, and concealment—all to drive sales and generate revenues in violation of U.S. law."

headline: Stock Market Today: Nasdaq Paces Slump As Rates Spike; Super Micro Crashes, But Dell Jumps (Live Coverage)
summary: The Nasdaq Composite and other major stock indexes fell Friday as interest rates climbed.  Super Micro Computer was an early loser on the stock market today, but bad news for the server maker was good news for Dell.  Selling was concentrated in the Russell 2000 small-cap index and Nasdaq composite, which were both down around 1.3%.

headline: Treasury yields surge, Super Micro stock plunges on chip accusations
summary: Yahoo Finance's John Hyland tracks Thursday's top moving stocks and biggest market stories in this Market Minute. US stocks (^DJI, ^GSPC, ^IXIC) are falling for a third straight session as investors stayed in risk-off mode amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and uncertainty around the global energy outlook. In the bond market (^TYX, ^TNX, ^FVX), short-term yields rose as traders reassessed rate expectations, shifting from anticipated cuts to potential hikes. Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shares plunged after US authorities charged a co-founder with illegally diverting Nvidia-powered (NVDA) servers to China, though the company was not named in the case.

content.title: Treasury yields surge, Super Micro stock plunges on chip accusations

Yahoo Finance's John Hyland tracks Thursday's top moving stocks and biggest market stories in this Market Minute.

US stocks (^DJI, ^GSPC, ^IXIC) are falling for a third straight session as investors stayed in risk-off mode amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and uncertainty around the global energy outlook.

In the bond market (^TYX, ^TNX, ^FVX), short-term yields rose as traders reassessed rate expectations, shifting from anticipated cuts to potential hikes.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shares plunged after US authorities charged a co-founder with illegally diverting Nvidia-powered (NVDA) servers to China, though the company was not named in the case.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) shares plunged after US authorities charged a co-founder with illegally diverting Nvidia-powered (NVDA) servers to China, though the company was not named in the case.

headline: Indictment of Super Micro Co-Founder Has a Long Backstory — Heard on the Street
summary: Super Micro Computer shares plunged after federal prosecutors unsealed an indictment against Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, a senior vice president and board member at the server maker.  Liaw resigned from Super Micro once before, amid an accounting scandal.  Liaw co-founded Super Micro in 1993 alongside Super Micro Chairman and Chief Executive Charles Liang and Liang’s wife, Sara Liu, another co-founder who is a senior vice president and director.

content.title: Indictment of Super Micro Co-Founder Has a Long Backstory — Heard on the Street
content.summary: Super Micro Computer shares plunged after federal prosecutors unsealed an indictment against Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, a senior vice president and board member at the server maker.  Liaw resigned from Super Micro once before, amid an accounting scandal.  Liaw co-founded Super Micro in 1993 alongside Super Micro Chairman and Chief Executive Charles Liang and Liang’s wife, Sara Liu, another co-founder who is a senior vice president and director.

headline: Dell Stock Is a Big Winner in the Super Micro Scandal. Why That May Be Overblown.
summary: Dell Technologies  stock surged Friday on news that had nothing to do with Dell.  The PC and server company got a boost after the Justice Department charged three individuals associated with  Super Micro Computer  Dell’s arch-rival, for allegedly skirting export-control laws by diverting $2.5 billion worth of U.S.-assembled servers to China.  One of the defendants is Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Super Micro’s co-founder and senior vice president, as well as a board member.

content.title: Dell Stock Is a Big Winner in the Super Micro Scandal. Why That May Be Overblown.
content.summary: Dell Technologies  stock surged Friday on news that had nothing to do with Dell.  The PC and server company got a boost after the Justice Department charged three individuals associated with  Super Micro Computer  Dell’s arch-rival, for allegedly skirting export-control laws by diverting $2.5 billion worth of U.S.-assembled servers to China.  One of the defendants is Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Super Micro’s co-founder and senior vice president, as well as a board member.

headline: Latest 'bad look' at Super Micro Computer batters stock, investor says
summary:
STORY: Super Micro shares sank more than 30% on Friday after U.S. prosecutors charged three people linked with the company, including its co-founder, with helping smuggle billions of dollars worth of AI technology to China.

U.S. prosecutors did not name Super Micro - a major AI server builder using Nvidia's chips - in the complaint. The company confirmed it was not named as a defendant in the case, and said it had cooperated with investigators.

The share drop could erase more than $5 billion from Super Micro's $18.49 billion market value, if they hold.

"This is one of many negative headlines that have involved the company in the last couple of years," Orczyk Sissel said. "In 2024, Ernst and Young resigned as their auditor due to irregularities they saw, that they were uncomfortable continuing.... In 2020 they were fined for accounting violations. It's just one thing after another."

"If what was happening with Super Micro right now was an isolated incident," she added, "it would not have impacted the stock as much as it has."

content.title: Latest 'bad look' at Super Micro Computer batters stock, investor says
content.summary:
STORY: Super Micro shares sank more than 30% on Friday after U.S. prosecutors charged three people linked with the company, including its co-founder, with helping smuggle billions of dollars worth of AI technology to China.

U.S. prosecutors did not name Super Micro - a major AI server builder using Nvidia's chips - in the complaint. The company confirmed it was not named as a defendant in the case, and said it had cooperated with investigators.

The share drop could erase more than $5 billion from Super Micro's $18.49 billion market value, if they hold.

"This is one of many negative headlines that have involved the company in the last couple of years," Orczyk Sissel said. "In 2024, Ernst and Young resigned as their auditor due to irregularities they saw, that they were uncomfortable continuing.... In 2020 they were fined for accounting violations. It's just one thing after another."

"If what was happening with Super Micro right now was an isolated incident," she added, "it would not have impacted the stock as much as it has."

The company also lifted its full-year guidance. The co-founder of Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw, was arrested for allegedly smuggling Nvidia (NVDA)-powered servers to China. Planet Labs (PL) stock is skyrocketing after the company beat fourth quarter revenue expectations.

Liaw and two others associated with Super Micro allegedly sold the hardware to an unnamed Southeast Asian company and coordinated for its ultimate shipment to Chinese customers.

headline: Super Micro's Liaw exits board following AI chip smuggling charges
summary: Super Micro Computer said on Friday that Yih-Shyan Liaw has resigned from its board, effective immediately, after the co-founder ‌was arrested by the U.S. Justice Department for helping smuggle billions ‌of dollars of AI chips to China.  Liaw's resignation was not the result of a disagreement with the company, Super Micro said in a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.  The Department of Justice on ‌Thursday charged Liaw, sales manager ⁠Ruei-Tsang Chang, and contractor Ting-Wei Sun with running a scheme to route U.S.-made servers through Taiwan to Southeast Asia.

content.title: Super Micro's Liaw exits board following AI chip smuggling charges
content.summary: Super Micro Computer said on Friday that Yih-Shyan Liaw has resigned from its board, effective immediately, after the co-founder ‌was arrested by the U.S. Justice Department for helping smuggle billions ‌of dollars of AI chips to China.  Liaw's resignation was not the result of a disagreement with the company, Super Micro said in a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.  The Department of Justice on ‌Thursday charged Liaw, sales manager ⁠Ruei-Tsang Chang, and contractor Ting-Wei Sun with running a scheme to route U.S.-made servers through Taiwan to Southeast Asia.
